{{Short description|2013 book by economist Thomas Piketty}}
{{infobox book
| name         = Capital in the Twenty-First Century
| title_orig   = Le Capital au XXI<sup>e</sup> siècle
| orig_lang_code    = fr
| translator   = [[Arthur Goldhammer]]
| image        = Capital in the Twenty-First Century (front cover).jpg
| caption      = Hardcover edition
| author       = [[Thomas Piketty]]
| language     = French
| subjects     = [[Political economy]], [[economic history]], [[economic inequality]], [[macroeconomics]]
| publisher    = {{ubl | [[Éditions du Seuil]] | [[Harvard University Press]] }}
| pub_date     = August 2013
| english_pub_date = April 15, 2014
| media_type   = Print ([[hardcover|hardback]])
| pages        = 696
| isbn =  978-0674430006
}}
'''''Capital in the Twenty-First Century''''' ({{langx|fr|'''Le Capital au XXI<sup>e</sup> siècle'''}}) is a book written by French economist [[Thomas Piketty]]. It focuses on [[economic inequality|wealth and income inequality]] in Europe and the United States since the 18th century. It was first published in French (as ''Le Capital au XXI<sup>e</sup> siècle'') in August 2013; an English translation by [[Arthur Goldhammer]] followed in April 2014.<ref>"[https://web.archive.org/web/20140530060756/http://www.businessweek.com/articles/2014-05-29/pikettys-capital-economists-inequality-ideas-are-all-the-rage Piketty's Capital: An Economist's Inequality Ideas Are All the Rage]" by [[Megan McArdle]], ''[[Bloomberg Businessweek]]'', May 29, 2014</ref>

The book's central thesis is that when the [[rate of return]] on [[capital (economics)|capital]] (''r'') is greater than the rate of [[economic growth]] (''g'') over the long term, the result is [[wealth concentration|concentration of wealth]], and this unequal distribution of [[wealth]] causes social and economic instability. Piketty proposes a global system of [[progressive tax|progressive]] [[wealth tax]]es to help reduce inequality and avoid the vast majority of wealth coming under the control of a tiny minority.

At the end of 2014, Piketty released a paper where he stated that he does not consider the relationship between the rate of return on capital and the rate of economic growth as the only or primary tool for considering changes in income and wealth inequality. He also noted that {{nowrap|r > g}} is not a useful tool for the discussion of rising inequality of labor income.<ref>{{Cite journal|url=http://piketty.pse.ens.fr/files/Piketty2015AER.pdf|title=About Capital in the 21st Century|last=Piketty|first=Thomas|date=31 December 2014|access-date=6 August 2017|journal=American Economic Review|volume=105|issue=5|pages=48–53|doi=10.1257/aer.p20151060|s2cid=14772228 }}</ref>

On May 18, 2014, the English edition reached number one on [[The New York Times Best Seller list|''The New York Times'' Best Seller list]] for best selling hardcover nonfiction<ref>{{cite web|title=Best Sellers May 18, 2014|work=[[The New York Times]]|url=https://www.nytimes.com/best-sellers-books/2014-05-18/hardcover-nonfiction/list.html|date=18 May 2014|access-date=29 January 2015}}</ref> and became the greatest sales success ever of academic publisher [[Harvard University Press]].<ref>[[Marc Tracy]] (24 April 2014). [https://newrepublic.com/article/117498/pikettys-capital-sold-out-harvard-press-scrambling Piketty's 'Capital': A Hit That Was, Wasn't, Then Was Again: How the French tome has rocked the tiny Harvard University Press]. ''[[The New Republic]].'' Retrieved 27 April 2014.</ref> As of January 2015, the book had sold 1.5 million copies in French, English, German, Chinese, and Spanish.<ref>{{cite news|title=French economist and best-selling author Thomas Piketty on Thursday refused France's highest honour – the Légion d'Honneur.|publisher=[[France 24]]|url=http://www.france24.com/en/20150101-best-selling-economist-piketty-refuses-france-top-honour|date=1 January 2015|access-date=29 January 2015}}</ref> The book is a worldwide success, with over 2.5 million copies sold by the end of 2017.<ref>[[Le Monde]] supp. économie et entreprise, 15th December 2017, p. 2</ref>

The book was adapted into a feature documentary film, directed by New Zealand filmmaker [[Justin Pemberton]],<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.screendaily.com/festivals/cannes/thomas-pikettys-capital-in-the-21st-century-set-for-doc-adaptation/5103665.article |title=Thomas Piketty's 'Capital In The 21st Century' set for doc adaptation |date=May 12, 2016 |access-date=July 31, 2016}}</ref>  and released in 2020.<ref name = OG>{{cite web |last=Gleiberman|first=Owen| url = https://variety.com/2020/film/reviews/capital-in-the-twenty-first-century-review-thomas-piketty-1234596239/ |date=2020-05-02|publisher = [[Variety (magazine)|Variety]] | website = variety.com | title = 'Capital in the Twenty-First Century': Film Review | access-date = 2024-09-01 }}</ref>{{TOC limit|3}}

==Publication and initial reception==
When first published in French in August 2013, Laurent Mauduit characterized it as "a political and theoretical bulldozer".<ref>Laurent Mauduit, "Piketty ausculte le capitalisme, ses contradictions et ses violentes inégalités", ''[[Mediapart]]'', 3 September 2013: {{lang|fr|"un bulldozer théorique et politique"}}</ref><ref name="Edsall" >[[Thomas B. Edsall]], [https://www.nytimes.com/2014/01/29/opinion/capitalism-vs-democracy.html "Capitalism vs.Democracy".] ''[[The New York Times]]'', 28 January 2014.</ref> As news spread of its thesis in the English-speaking world, [[Paul Krugman]] hailed it as a landmark,<ref name = "Krugman1" >[[Paul Krugman]].  [https://www.nytimes.com/2014/03/28/opinion/krugman-americas-taxation-tradition.html "America's Taxation Tradition"], in ''[[The New York Times]]'', 27 March 2014 .</ref> while former senior [[World Bank]] economist [[Branko Milanović]] considers it "one of the watershed books in economic thinking".<ref name="Cassidy" >[[John Cassidy (journalist)|John Cassidy]], [http://www.newyorker.com/arts/critics/books/2014/03/31/140331crbo_books_cassidy "Forces of Divergence: Is surging inequality endemic to capitalism?"], in ''[[The New Yorker]]'', 31 March 2014</ref> In response to widespread curiosity abroad aroused by reviews of the original French edition published by [[Seuil]] in September 2013, it was translated rapidly into English and its publication date was pushed forward to March 2014 by [[Harvard University Press|Belknap]]. It proved an overnight sensation<ref name = "The Economist2">[https://www.economist.com/news/leaders/21601512-thomas-pikettys-blockbuster-book-great-piece-scholarship-poor-guide-policy&rct=j&frm=1&q=&esrc=s "Thomas Piketty's blockbuster book is a great piece of scholarship, but a poor guide to policy"], ''[[Economist]]'' 3 May 2014.</ref> and ousted [[Michael Lewis]]'s financial exposé, ''[[Flash Boys|Flash Boys: Cracking the Money Code]]'', from the top of the US best-seller list.<ref>[[John Lanchester]] [http://www.lrb.co.uk/v36/n11/john-lanchester/scalpers-inc "Flash Boys"] in ''[[London Review of Books]]'' Vol. 36 No. 11, 5 June 2014 pp. 7–9</ref> Within a year of its publication, [[Stephanie Kelton]] spoke of a "Piketty phenomenon",<ref name="Moore" >Heidi Moore, [https://www.theguardian.com/money/2014/sep/21/-sp-thomas-piketty-bestseller-why "Why is Thomas Piketty's 700-page book a bestseller?"], ''[[The Guardian]]'' 21 September 2014</ref> and in Germany three books had been published specifically dealing with Piketty's critique.<ref>Heinz-J. Bontrup, [http://www.labournet.de/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/piketty_bontrup2.pdf ''Pikettys Krisen-Analyse. Warum die Reichen immer reicher und die Armen immer ärmer werden''], pad-verlag. Bergkamen 2014, {{ISBN|978-3885152606}}.</ref><ref>Albert F. Reiterer, [http://www.labournet.de/wp-content/uploads/2014/06/piketty_reiterer2.pdf ''Der Piketty-Hype – "The great U-Turn". Piketty's Kapital und die neoliberale Vermögenskonzentration''], pad-Verlag, Bergkamen 2014, {{ISBN|978-3885152590}}.</ref><ref>Stephan Kaufmann, [[Ingo Stützle]], [http://www.bertz-fischer.de/product_info.php%3FcPath%3D1_33%26products_id%3D447&rct=j&frm=1&q=&esrc=s  ''Kapitalismus: Die ersten 200 Jahre. Thomas Pikettys "Das Kapital im 21. Jahrhundert": Einführung, Debatte, Kritik''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305023417/http://bertz-fischer.de/product_info.php?cpath=1_33&products_id=447&rct=j&frm=1&q=&esrc=s |date=2016-03-05 }}, Bertz + Fischer Verlag, Berlin 2014, {{ISBN|978-3865057303}}.</ref>

==Contents==
The central thesis of the book is that [[Economic inequality|inequality]] is not an accident, but rather a feature of [[capitalism]] and can only be reversed through [[state interventionism]].<ref name="cooper">{{cite news |author=Ryan Cooper |title=Why everyone is talking about Thomas Piketty's Capital in the Twenty-First Century |url=http://theweek.com/article/index/258666/why-everyone-is-talking-about-thomas-pikettys-capital-in-the-twenty-first-century |newspaper=[[The Week]] |date=March 25, 2014 }}</ref> The book thus argues that, unless capitalism is reformed, the very [[democracy|democratic]] order will be threatened.<ref name="cooper"/>

Piketty bases his argument on a formula that relates the rate of [[return on capital]] (''r'') to [[economic growth]] (''g''), where ''r'' includes [[profit (economics)|profits]], [[dividend]]s, [[interest]], [[economic rent|rent]]s, and other [[income]] from capital and ''g'' is measured as growth of society's income or [[output (economics)|output]]. He argues that when the rate of growth is low, then wealth tends to accumulate more quickly from ''r'' than from [[Labour economics|labor]] and tends to accumulate more among the top 10% and 1%, increasing inequality. Thus, the fundamental force for divergence and greater [[wealth inequality]] can be summed up in [[Inequality (mathematics)|the inequality]] ''r > g''. He analyzes [[inheritance]] from the perspective of the same formula.

[[File:Top1percentIncomePiketty.png|thumb|Income inequality as measured by the income of the top 1% in several countries. Inequality tended to drop in the middle of the century but has increased in the past several decades.]]

The book argues that there was a trend towards higher inequality that was reversed between 1930 and 1975 due to unique circumstances: the two [[world wars]], the [[Great Depression]], and a [[debt]]-fueled [[recession]] destroyed much wealth, particularly that owned by [[elite|the elite]].<ref name="Pearlstein">{{cite news |author=Steven Pearlstein |title='Capital in the Twenty-first Century' by Thomas Piketty |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/capital-in-the-twenty-first-century-by-thomas-piketty/2014/03/28/ea75727a-a87a-11e3-8599-ce7295b6851c_story.html |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=March 28, 2014 }}</ref> These events prompted governments to undertake steps towards redistributing income, especially in the post–World War II period. The fast, worldwide [[economic growth]] of that time began to reduce the importance of inherited wealth in the [[global economy]].<ref name="Pearlstein"/>

The book argues that the world today is returning towards "[[Inheritance|patrimonial capitalism]]", in which much of the [[economy]] is dominated by inherited wealth: the power of this economic class is increasing, threatening to create an [[oligarchy]].<ref name="Krugman">{{cite news |author=Paul Krugman |title=Wealth Over Work |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/03/24/opinion/krugman-wealth-over-work.html |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=March 23, 2014 }}</ref> Piketty cites novels by [[Honoré de Balzac]], [[Jane Austen]], and [[Henry James]]<ref name="Pearlstein"/> to describe the rigid [[social class|class structure]] based on [[capital accumulation|accumulated capital]] that existed in England and France in the early 1800s.

Piketty proposes that a progressive annual global [[wealth tax]] of up to 2%, combined with a [[progressive tax|progressive]] [[income tax]] reaching as high as 80%, would reduce [[economic inequality|inequality]],<ref name="Pearlstein"/> although he says that such a tax "would be politically impossible".<ref name="bigger">{{cite news |url=https://www.economist.com/news/finance-and-economics/21601567-wonky-book-inequality-becomes-blockbuster-bigger-marx |title=Bigger Than Marx. |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |date=May 3, 2014 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |access-date=December 23, 2015}}</ref>

[[File:Piketty_fig10_10.png|thumb|Piketty believes the growth rate will once again fall below the rate of return, and the twentieth century will be an aberration in terms of inequality.<ref>{{cite magazine|url=http://www.newyorker.com/news/john-cassidy/pikettys-inequality-story-in-six-charts|title=Piketty's Inequality Story In Six Charts|author=John Cassidy|date=March 26, 2014|magazine=[[The New Yorker]]}}</ref>]]

Without tax adjustment, Piketty predicts a world of low economic growth and extreme inequality. His data show that over long periods of time, the average [[return on investment]] outpaces [[productivity]]-based income by a wide margin.<ref name="Pearlstein"/> He dismisses the idea that bursts of productivity resulting from technological advances can be relied on to return sustained economic growth; we should not expect "a more just and rational order" to arise based on "caprices of technology",<ref name="Pearlstein"/> and return on investment can increase when technology can be substituted for people.<ref name="bigger"/>

==Reception==
The book's exceptional success was widely attributed to "being about the right subject at the right time", as ''[[The Economist]]'' put it. Piketty himself recognized there is a common sense that "inequality and wealth in the United States have been widening." The [[Occupy movement]]'s "[[We are the 99%]]" slogan made talk of inequality "the zeitgeist of our age – an age of seemingly permanent crisis and austerity," as Adam Booth put it.<ref>{{cite web |first=Adam |last=Booth |title=Piketty's Capital and the Spectre of Inequality |work=In Defence of Marxism |url=http://www.marxist.com/pikettys-capital-and-the-spectre-of-inequality.htm |date=29 August 2014 |access-date=1 May 2015}}</ref>

British author [[Paul Mason (journalist)|Paul Mason]] dismissed charges of "soft Marxism" as "completely misplaced", noting that Marx described social relations trying to unveil capitalism's inner tendencies, where Piketty solely relies on social categories and historical data. Piketty rather "placed an unexploded bomb within mainstream, classical economics," he concludes.<ref>{{cite web |first=Paul |last=Mason |title=Thomas Piketty's Capital: everything you need to know about the surprise bestseller |url=https://www.theguardian.com/books/2014/apr/28/thomas-piketty-capital-surprise-bestseller |date=28 April 2014 |work=[[The Guardian]] |location=London |access-date=1 May 2015}}</ref>

Other scholars have built upon Piketty's work, such as historian [[Walter Scheidel]], who concurs with Piketty in his own study of inequality (''The Great Leveler'', 2017) that the gap will continue to widen as the decades pass but contends that Piketty's solutions are untenable.<ref>{{cite news |last=Taylor |first= Matt |date=February 22, 2017 |title=One Recipe for a More Equal World: Mass Death |url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/one-recipe-for-a-more-equal-world-mass-death/ |work=[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]] |access-date=April 7, 2017 }}</ref>

===Praise===
[[File:Piketty in Cambridge 3 crop.jpg|thumb|The author [[Thomas Piketty]]]]

[[Paul Krugman]] called the book a "magnificent, sweeping meditation on inequality"<ref name="gilded age">Paul Krugman (May 8, 2014). [http://www.nybooks.com/articles/archives/2014/may/08/thomas-piketty-new-gilded-age/ "Why We're in a New Gilded Age"]. ''[[The New York Review of Books]]''. Retrieved April 14, 2014.</ref> and "the most important economics book of the year{{snd}}and maybe of the decade."<ref name="Krugman"/> He distinguishes the book from other [[bestseller]]s on [[economics]] as it constitutes "serious, discourse-changing scholarship".<ref>Paul Krugman (April 24, 2014). [https://www.nytimes.com/2014/04/25/opinion/krugman-the-piketty-panic.html "The Piketty Panic"]. ''[[The New York Times]]''. Retrieved April 26, 2014.</ref> Krugman also wrote:<ref name="gilded age" />

<blockquote>At a time when the concentration of wealth and income in the hands of a few has resurfaced as a central political issue, Piketty doesn't just offer invaluable documentation of what is happening, with unmatched historical depth. He also offers what amounts to a unified field theory of inequality, one that integrates economic growth, the distribution of income between capital and labor, and the distribution of wealth and income among individuals into a single frame.&nbsp;... ''Capital in the Twenty-First Century'' is an extremely important book on all fronts. Piketty has transformed our economic discourse; we'll never talk about wealth and inequality the same way we used to.</blockquote>

[[Steven Pearlstein]] called it a "triumph of [[economic history]] over [[economic model|the theoretical, mathematical modeling]] that has come to dominate the economics profession in recent years", but also added: "Piketty's analysis of the past is more impressive than his predictions for the future are convincing."<ref name="Pearlstein"/>

[[Branko Milanović]], a former senior [[economist]] at the [[World Bank]], called the book "one of the watershed books in economic thinking."<ref>[[John Cassidy (journalist)|John Cassidy]] (March 31, 2014). [http://www.newyorker.com/arts/critics/books/2014/03/31/140331crbo_books_cassidy?currentPage=all "Forces of Divergence: Is Surging Inequality Endemic to Capitalism?"] ''[[The New Yorker]]''. Retrieved April 13, 2014.</ref><ref>Branko Milanovic (October 2013). [http://mpra.ub.uni-muenchen.de/52384/1/MPRA_paper_52384.pdf ''The return of "patrimonial capitalism": review of Thomas Piketty's "Capital in the 21st century"''], Munich Personal RePEc Archive. Retrieved April 20, 2014.</ref>

British historian [[Andrew Hussey]] called the book "epic" and "groundbreaking" and argues that it proves "scientifically" that the [[Occupy movement]] was correct in its assertion that "capitalism isn't working".<ref>[[Andrew Hussey]] (April 12, 2014). [https://www.theguardian.com/books/2014/apr/13/occupy-right-capitalism-failed-world-french-economist-thomas-piketty Occupy was right: capitalism has failed the world]. ''[[The Guardian]]''. Retrieved April 21, 2014.</ref>

According to [[Robert Solow]], Piketty has made a "new and powerful contribution to an old topic: as long as the rate of return exceeds the rate of growth, the income and wealth of the rich will grow faster than the typical income from work".<ref>Robert M. Solow. [https://newrepublic.com/article/117429/capital-twenty-first-century-thomas-piketty-reviewed Thomas Piketty Is Right]. ''[[The New Republic]].''</ref>

French historian and political scientist [[Emmanuel Todd]] called ''Capital in the Twenty-First Century'' a "masterpiece" and "a seminal book on the economic and social evolution of the planet".<ref>Emmanuel Todd (September 14, 2013). [http://www.marianne.net/Piketty-decrypte-le-come-back-des-heritiers_a231808.html Piketty décrypte le come-back des héritiers] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140424094601/http://www.marianne.net/Piketty-decrypte-le-come-back-des-heritiers_a231808.html |date=2014-04-24 }}. ''[[Marianne (magazine)|Marianne]].''</ref>

The book has been described as "a political and theoretical bulldozer" in the French press.<ref>[[Thomas B. Edsall]] (January 28, 2014). [https://www.nytimes.com/2014/01/29/opinion/capitalism-vs-democracy.html?_r=0 Capitalism vs. Democracy]. ''[[The New York Times]].'' Retrieved April 15, 2014.</ref>

''[[The Economist]]'' wrote: "A modern surge in inequality has new economists wondering, as [[Marx]] and [[David Ricardo|Ricardo]] did, which forces may be stopping the fruits of capitalism from being more widely distributed. ''Capital in the Twenty-First Century''&nbsp;... is an authoritative guide to the question."<ref>[https://www.economist.com/news/finance-and-economics/21592635-revisiting-old-argument-about-impact-capitalism-all-men-are-created/ All Men Are Created Unequal], [[The Economist]], Print Edition, January 4, 2014.</ref>

[[Will Hutton]] wrote: "Like [[Milton Friedman|Friedman]], Piketty is a man for the times. For 1970s anxieties about inflation substitute today's concerns about the emergence of the [[plutocratic]] rich and their impact on economy and society.&nbsp;... the current level of rising wealth inequality, set to grow still further, now imperils the very future of capitalism. He has proved it."<ref>Will Hutton (April 12, 2014). [https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2014/apr/12/capitalism-isnt-working-thomas-piketty Capitalism simply isn't working and here are the reasons why]. ''[[The Guardian]].'' Retrieved April 22, 2014.</ref>

[[Clive Crook]], while being strongly critical of the book, described it as earning more praise than any other economics book in decades. He suggests it was "greeted with... erotic intensity" because of a demand for "scholarly respectability" that would affirm the belief that inequality is, quoting [[John Cassidy (journalist)|John Cassidy]], "the defining issue of our era".<ref name="allwrong">{{cite web|title=The Most Important Book Ever Is All Wrong|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2014-04-20/the-most-important-book-ever-is-all-wrong|publisher=[[Bloomberg View]]|access-date=22 April 2014|archive-date=2016-04-23|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160423001832/http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2014-04-20/the-most-important-book-ever-is-all-wrong|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="Cassidy"/>

In the introduction to the essay collection ''After Piketty'' (2017), Piketty is praised for arguing, before [[2016 United States presidential election|Donald Trump's election]], that those with property will dominate the twenty-first century political economy and set in motion forces to keep the rate of profit high enough to create plutocracy.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2017-07-16|title=After Piketty|url=https://www.evatt.org.au/post/after-piketty|access-date=2020-08-28|website=Evatt Foundation|language=en}}</ref>

===Criticism===
====Critique of the normative content====
One strand of critique faults Piketty for placing inequality at the center of analysis without any reflection on why it matters.

According to ''Financial Times'' columnist [[Martin Wolf]], he merely assumes that inequality matters, but never explains why. He only demonstrates that it exists and how it worsens.<ref>Martin Wolf, [http://www.ft.com/intl/cms/s/2/0c6e9302-c3e2-11e3-a8e0-00144feabdc0.html#axzz2yz1aCVku "''Capital in the Twenty-First Century'', by Thomas Piketty"], Financial Times, April 15, 2014, .</ref> Or as his colleague [[Clive Crook]] put it: "Aside from its other flaws, ''Capital in the 21st Century'' invites readers to believe not just that inequality is important, but that nothing else matters. This book wants you to worry about low growth in the coming decades not because that would mean a slower rise in [[living standard]]s, but because it might&nbsp;... worsen inequality."<ref name="allwrong"/>

Icelandic professor [[Hannes Hólmsteinn Gissurarson|Hannes H. Gissurarson]] asserts that Piketty is replacing American philosopher [[John Rawls]] as the essential thinker of the left.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Gissurarson|first=Hannes H.|date=2014|editor-last=Piketty|editor-first=Thomas|editor2-last=Goldhammer|editor2-first=Arthur|title=A Latter-Day Jacobin with a Lot of Data|jstor=10.5325/jaynrandstud.14.2.0281|journal=The Journal of Ayn Rand Studies|volume=14|issue=2|pages=281–290|doi=10.5325/jaynrandstud.14.2.0281|s2cid=169350719}}</ref> In addition to questioning common measures of wealth distribution, he also criticizes Piketty for being, unlike Rawls, "much more concerned with the rich than with the poor". Hannes admits that the "rapid rise in the income of the super-rich of the world" is happening but doesn't view this trend as being a problem so long as the poor do not get poorer.<ref>{{cite journal|first=Hannes H.|last=Gissurarson|author-link=Hannes Hólmsteinn Gissurarson|title=A Latter-Day Jacobin with a Lot of Data|journal=The Journal of Ayn Rand Studies|pages=281–290|url=https://www.academia.edu/9709958|volume=14|issue=2|date=1 December 2014|access-date=30 January 2015|doi=10.5325/jaynrandstud.14.2.0281|s2cid=169350719}}</ref>

====Methodological critique====
[[Lawrence Summers]] criticizes Piketty for underestimating the [[diminishing returns]] on capital, which he believes will offset the return on capital and hence set an upper limit to inequality. Summers challenges another of Piketty's assumptions: that returns to wealth are largely reinvested. A declining ratio of saving to wealth would also set upper limits on inequality in society.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2014/05/thomas-piketty-is-right-about-the-past-and-wrong-about-the-future/370994/|title=Thomas Piketty Is Right About the Past and Wrong About the Future|date=May 16, 2014|work=[[The Atlantic]]|last=Summers|first=Lawrence}}</ref> Of 400 wealthiest Americans in 1982, only one in ten remained on the list in 2012, and an increasing share of wealthiest people have not increased their fortunes. Moreover, top 1% incomes are now mostly salaries, not capital incomes. Most other economists explain the rise of top 1% incomes by globalization and technological change.<ref name=ls>[http://www.democracyjournal.org/32/the-inequality-puzzle.php The Inequality Puzzle], Lawrence H. Summers, [[Democracy Journal]], Issue #33, Summer 2014.</ref>

[[James K. Galbraith]] criticizes Piketty for using "an [[empirical]] measure that is unrelated to productive [[physical capital]] and whose dollar value depends, in part, on the return on capital. Where does the rate of return come from? Piketty never says". Galbraith also says: "Despite its great ambitions, his book is not the accomplished work of high theory that its title, length and reception (so far) suggest."<ref name="Galbraith">{{cite web |first=James K. |last=Galbraith |url=http://www.dissentmagazine.org/article/kapital-for-the-twenty-first-century |title=''Kapital'' for the Twenty-First Century? |work=[[Dissent (American magazine)|Dissent]] |date=Spring 2014 |access-date=April 28, 2014}}</ref>

[[Daron Acemoglu]] and [[James A. Robinson (economist)|James A. Robinson]] used the economic histories of Sweden and South Africa to show that social inequality depends much more on institutional factors than Piketty's factors like the difference between rate of return and growth. Cross-country analysis also shows that the top 1%'s share of income does not depend on that difference. The professors write that general laws, which is how they characterize Piketty's postulations, "are unhelpful as a guide to understand the past or predict the future because they ignore the central role of political and economic institutions in shaping the evolution of [[technology]] and the distribution of resources in a society".<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Acemoglu|first1=Daron|last2=Robinson|first2=James A.|title=The Rise and Decline of General Laws of Capitalism|journal=[[Journal of Economic Perspectives]]|date=2015|volume=29|issue=1|pages=3–28|url=http://economics.mit.edu/files/11348|doi=10.1257/jep.29.1.3 |doi-access=free|citeseerx=10.1.1.687.2487|hdl=1721.1/113636|s2cid=14001669|access-date=2016-11-10|archive-date=2017-02-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170213070624/http://economics.mit.edu/files/11348|url-status=dead}}</ref> Per Krusell and Anthony Smith criticise Piketty's second law as implausible based on empirically supported theories of savings and that the data supports theories opposed to Piketty's.<ref>Krusell, Per, and Anthony A. Smith Jr. "Is Piketty's 'second law of capitalism' fundamental?." ''Journal of Political Economy'' 123, no. 4 (2015): 725–748.</ref>

[[Paul Romer]] criticises that while the data and empirical analysis is presented with admirable clarity and precision, the theory is presented in less detail. In his opinion the work was written with the attitude "Empirical work is science; theory is entertainment" and therefore an example for [[mathiness]].<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Romer | first1 = Paul | year = 2015 | title = Mathiness in the Theory of Economic Growth | url = http://www.docdroid.net/10gny/aer2ep20151066.pdf.html | journal = American Economic Review: Papers and Proceedings | volume = 105 | issue = 5| pages = 89–93 | doi = 10.1257/aer.p20151066 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Lawrence Blume and Steven Durlauf criticized the book in the ''Journal of Political Economy'' for being "unpersuasive when it turns from description to analysis... Both of us are very liberal (in the contemporary as opposed to classical sense), and we regard ourselves as egalitarians. We are therefore disturbed that Piketty has undermined the egalitarian case with weak empirical, analytical, and ethical arguments."<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://bostonreview.net/class-inequality/marshall-steinbaum-why-are-economists-giving-piketty-cold-shoulder|title=Why Are Economists Giving Piketty the Cold Shoulder?|last=Renner|first=Nausicaa|date=2017-05-10|website=Boston Review|access-date=2017-05-12}}</ref>

====Critique of Piketty's basic concepts====
German economist [[Stefan Homburg]] criticizes Piketty for equating wealth with capital. Homburg argues that wealth does not only embrace [[capital good]]s in the sense of produced [[means of production]], but also [[land]] and other [[natural resource]]s. Homburg argues that observed increases in wealth income ratios reflect rising land prices and not an accumulation of [[machinery]].<ref>{{cite journal |first=Stefan |last=Homburg |year=2015 |title=Critical remarks on Piketty's ''Capital in the Twenty-first Century'' |journal=[[Applied Economics (journal)|Applied Economics]] |volume=47 |issue=14 |pages=1401–1406 |doi=10.1080/00036846.2014.997927  |s2cid=7243323 |doi-access=free }}</ref> [[Joseph E. Stiglitz]] endorses this view, pointing out that "a large fraction of the increase in wealth is an increase in the value of [[land (economics)|land]], not in the amount of capital goods".<ref>{{citation|title=Thomas Piketty gets income inequality wrong|url= http://www.salon.com/2015/01/02/joseph_stiglitz_thomas_piketty_gets_income_inequality_wrong_partner/|website=Salon.com|first=Joseph E.|last=Stiglitz|publisher=Salon Media Group Inc.|location=San Francisco|date=January 3, 2015|access-date=2015-04-17}}</ref>

This idea is furthered by Matthew Rognlie, then a graduate student at [[Massachusetts Institute of Technology|MIT]], who published a paper in March 2015 with the Brookings Institution that argues that Piketty did not take the effects of depreciation into account enough in his analysis of the growing importance of capital. Rognlie also found that "surging house prices are almost entirely responsible for growing returns on capital."<ref>{{cite journal |first=Matthew |last=Rognlie |title=Deciphering the Fall and Rise in the Net Capital Share: Accumulation, or Scarcity? |journal=Brookings Papers on Economic Activity |volume= |date=Spring 2015 |pages=1–54 |url= http://www.brookings.edu/~/media/projects/bpea/spring-2015/2015a_rognlie.pdf |jstor=43684097}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Through the roof |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |date=March 28, 2015 |url=https://www.economist.com/news/finance-and-economics/21647349-rising-house-prices-may-be-chiefly-responsible-rising-inequality-through }}</ref> A similar critique was made by Odran Bonnet, et al. in "Does housing capital contribute to inequality? A comment on Thomas Piketty's Capital in the 21st Century published in 2014."<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bonnet |first=Odran |date=April 17, 2014 |title=Does housing capital contribute to inequality? A comment on Thomas Piketty's Capital in the 21st Century |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/35310497.pdf |journal=Sciences Po Economics Discussion Papers}}</ref>

[[Marxist]] academic [[David Harvey]], while praising the book for demolishing "the widely-held view that [[free market]] capitalism spreads the wealth around and that it is the great bulwark for the defense of [[individual liberties]] and freedoms," is largely critical of Piketty for, among other things, his "mistaken definition of capital", which Harvey describes as:<ref name="Harvey 2014">[[David Harvey]] (May 20, 2014). [http://inthesetimes.com/article/16722/taking_on_capital_without_marx Taking on 'Capital' Without Marx: What Thomas Piketty misses in his critique of capitalism]. ''[[In These Times (publication)|In These Times]].'' Retrieved May 20, 2014.</ref>
<blockquote>...&nbsp;a process, not a thing&nbsp;... a process of [[money circulation|circulation]] in which money is used to make more money often, but not exclusively through the exploitation of [[labor power]]. Piketty defines capital as the stock of all assets held by private individuals, corporations and governments that can be traded in the [[market (economics)|market]] no matter whether these assets are being used or not.</blockquote>

Harvey further argues that Piketty's "proposals as to the remedies for the inequalities are naïve if not [[utopian]]. And he has certainly not produced a working model for capital of the twenty-first century. For that, we still need Marx or his modern-day equivalent". Harvey also takes Piketty to task for dismissing Marx's ''[[Das Kapital]]'' without ever having read it.<ref name="Harvey 2014"/>

[[International Monetary Fund|IMF]] economist Carlos Góes researched the basic thesis put forth by the book – that when the [[rate of return]] on [[capital (economics)|capital]] (''r'') is greater than the rate of [[economic growth]] (''g'') over the long term, the result is [[wealth concentration|concentration of wealth]] – and found no empirical support for it; in fact, an opposite trend was identified in 75% of the countries studied in depth.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.imf.org/external/pubs/ft/wp/2016/wp16160.pdf|title=Testing Piketty's Hypothesis on the Drivers of Income Inequality: Evidence from Panel VARs with Heterogeneous Dynamics|last=Góes|first=Carlos|publisher=[[International Monetary Fund]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://blogs.wsj.com/economics/2016/08/05/no-empirical-evidence-for-thomas-pikettys-inequality-theory-imf-economist-argues/|title='No Empirical Evidence' for Thomas Piketty's Inequality Theory, IMF Economist Argues|last=Talley|first=Ian|newspaper=Wall Street Journal|date=5 August 2016|access-date=2016-08-13}}</ref> Piketty's response<ref>{{Cite web |title=Thomas Piketty répond à l'étude du FMI qui contredisait son livre|work=[[Les Échos (France)|Les Échos]] |date=September 20, 2016 |url=https://www.lesechos.fr/20/09/2016/lesechos.fr/0211309465129_thomas-piketty-repond-a-l-etude-du-fmi-qui-contredisait-son-livre.htm}}</ref> noted, however, that Góes used measures of income inequality rather than wealth inequality, and inappropriately took the interest rate on sovereign debt as his index of the rate of return on capital, which makes his results not commensurate with those of Piketty's study.

====Critique of the proposed measures====
In a similar vein, philosopher Nicholas Vrousalis faults Piketty's remedies for misconstruing the kind of political "counter-agency" required to remove the inequalities Piketty criticizes and for thinking that they are compatible with capitalism.<ref>{{citation |last=Vrousalis |first=Nicholas |author-link=Nicholas Vrousalis| date= September 1, 2014 |ssrn = 2495840 |title= Piketty's Grandchildren | publisher = [[Social Science Research Network]] }}</ref>

====Critique of the conventional paradigm====
Norwegian economist and journalist Maria Reinertsen compares the book to the 2014 book ''[[If Women Counted#Counting on Marilyn Waring: New Advances in Feminist Economics|Counting on Marilyn Waring: New Advances in Feminist Economics]], ''by [[Ailsa McKay]] and [[Margunn Bjørnholt]], arguing that, "while ''Capital in the Twenty-First Century'' barely touches the boundaries of the discipline in its focus on the rich, ''Counting on Marilyn Waring'' challenges most limits of what economists should care about".<ref>{{citation |first= Maria |last= Reinertsen |title=Bør morsmelk regnes med i bruttonasjonalprodukt? (English: "Should milk be included in the gross domestic product?") | journal =[[Morgenbladet]] |number = 26  |pages = 6–7|date = July 4, 2014 |url=http://www.margunnbjornholt.no/wp-content/uploads/2014/07/morgenbladet2014.pdf| access-date= January 23, 2015}}</ref>

===Allegation of data errors===
On May 23, 2014, Chris Giles, economics editor of the ''[[Financial Times]]'' (''FT''), identified what he claims are "unexplained errors" in Piketty's data, in particular regarding wealth inequality increases since the 1970s.<ref>{{cite web|first=Chris|last=Giles|title=Thomas Piketty's exhaustive inequality data turn out to be flawed|work=[[Financial Times]]|url=http://www.ft.com/intl/cms/s/0/c9ce1a54-e281-11e3-89fd-00144feabdc0.html|date=May 23, 2014|access-date=May 23, 2014}}</ref> The ''FT'' wrote in part:<ref>{{cite web |first=Jordan |last=Weissmann |title=Financial Times: Piketty's Data Is Full of Errors |url=http://www.slate.com/blogs/moneybox/2014/05/23/financial_times_on_piketty_his_data_is_wrong.html |work=Slate |date=May 23, 2014 |access-date=May 25, 2014}}</ref>
<blockquote>
The data&nbsp;... contain a series of errors that skew his findings. The ''FT'' found mistakes and unexplained entries in his spreadsheets, similar to those which last year undermined the work on [[public debt]] and growth of [[Carmen Reinhart]] and [[Kenneth Rogoff]].

The central theme of Prof Piketty's work is that wealth inequalities are heading back up to levels last seen before [[World War I]]. The investigation undercuts this claim, indicating there is little evidence in Prof Piketty's original sources to bear out the thesis that an increasing share of total wealth is held by the richest few.
</blockquote>

Piketty wrote a response defending his findings and arguing that subsequent studies (he links to [[Emmanuel Saez]] and [[Gabriel Zucman]]'s March 2014 presentation, ''The Distribution of US Wealth, Capital Income and Returns since 1913''<ref>{{Cite web |last=Saez |first=Emmanuel |last2=Zucman |first2=Gabriel |date=2014-10-01 |title=Wealth Inequality in the United States since 1913 |url=https://gabriel-zucman.eu/files/SaezZucman2014Slides.pdf}}</ref>) confirm his conclusions about increasing wealth inequality and actually show a greater increase in inequality for the United States than he does in his book.<ref>Thomas Piketty (May 23, 2014). [http://blogs.ft.com/money-supply/2014/05/23/piketty-response-to-ft-data-concerns/ Piketty response to FT data concerns]. ''[[Financial Times]].'' May 23, 2014</ref> In an interview with ''[[Agence France-Presse]]'', he accused the ''Financial Times'' of "dishonest criticism" and said that the paper "is being ridiculous because all of its contemporaries recognise that the biggest fortunes have grown faster".<ref name="JenniferRankin">Jennifer Rankin (May 26, 2014). [https://www.theguardian.com/business/2014/may/26/thomas-piketty-financial-times-dishonest-criticism-economics-book-inequality Thomas Piketty accuses Financial Times of dishonest criticism]. ''[[The Guardian]].'' Retrieved May 26, 2014.</ref>

The accusation received wide press coverage.<ref>Mark Gongloff (May 23, 2014). [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/05/23/piketty-data-flaw_n_5380947.html Thomas Piketty's Inequality Data Contains 'Unexplained' Errors: FT]. ''[[The Huffington Post]].'' Retrieved May 23, 2014.</ref><ref>Kevin Drum (May 23, 2014). [https://www.motherjones.com/kevin-drum/2014/05/chris-giles-challenges-thomas-pikettys-data-analysis Chris Giles Challenges Thomas Piketty's Data Analysis]. ''[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]].'' Retrieved May 23, 2014.</ref><ref>{{cite web |first=Neil |last=Irwin |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/05/24/upshot/did-piketty-get-his-math-wrong.html |title=Did Thomas Piketty Get His Math Wrong? |work=[[The New York Times]] |date=May 23, 2014 |access-date=May 25, 2014}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |first=Jamie |last=Doward |title=Thomas Piketty's economic data 'came out of thin air' |url=https://www.theguardian.com/business/2014/may/24/thomas-picketty-economics-data-errors |work=The Guardian |date=May 24, 2014 |access-date=May 25, 2014}}</ref> Some sources said the ''Financial Times'' has overstated its case. For example, ''[[The Economist]]'', a sister publication to the ''Financial Times'' at that time, wrote:<ref>{{cite news |last=R.A. |url=https://www.economist.com/blogs/freeexchange/2014/05/inequality-0 |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |title=A Piketty problem? |date=May 24, 2014 |access-date=May 25, 2014}}</ref>
<blockquote>
Mr Giles's analysis is impressive, and one certainly hopes that further work by Mr Giles, Mr Piketty or others will clarify whether mistakes have been made, how they came to be introduced and what their effects are. Based on the information Mr Giles has provided so far, however, the analysis does not seem to support many of the allegations made by the ''FT'', or the conclusion that the book's argument is wrong.
</blockquote>

Scott Winship, a sociologist at the [[Manhattan Institute for Policy Research]] and critic of Piketty, asserts the allegations are not "significant for the fundamental question of whether Piketty's thesis is right or not&nbsp;... It's hard to think Piketty did something unethical when he put it up there for people like me to delve into his figures and find something that looks sketchy&nbsp;... Piketty has been as good or better than anyone at both making all his data available and documenting what he does generally".<ref name="JenniferRankin"/>

In addition to Winship, the economists [[Alan Reynolds (economist)|Alan Reynolds]], [[Justin Wolfers]], [[James D. Hamilton|James Hamilton]] and [[Gabriel Zucman]] claim that ''FT'''s assertions go too far.<ref name="Wealth Data Worthless">Alan Reynolds (July 9, 2014). [http://www.cato.org/publications/commentary/why-pikettys-wealth-data-are-worthless Why Piketty's Wealth Data Are Worthless]. ''[[The Wall Street Journal]].'' Retrieved July 11, 2014.</ref><ref name="Piketty Takedown">Mark Gongloff (May 27, 2014). [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/05/27/piketty-not-wrong_n_5397358.html Economists Rip Apart FT's Piketty Takedown]. ''[[The Huffington Post]].'' Retrieved May 27, 2014.</ref> Paul Krugman noted that "anyone imagining that the whole notion of rising wealth inequality has been refuted is almost surely going to be disappointed".<ref name="Piketty Takedown"/> [[Emmanuel Saez]], a colleague of Piketty and one of the economists cited by Giles to discredit him, stated that "Piketty's choice and judgement were quite good" and that his own research supports Piketty's thesis.<ref>Ryan Grim (May 27, 2014). [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/05/27/thomas-piketty-ft_n_5400214.html?ncid=fcbklnkushpmg00000015&ir=Business The Economists FT Relied On For Its Thomas Piketty Takedown Don't Buy It]. ''[[The Huffington Post]].'' Retrieved May 28, 2014.</ref> Piketty released a full point-by-point rebuttal on his website.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Piketty |first=Thomas |date=2014-05-28 |title=Technical appendix of the book « Capital in the twenty-first century» Appendix to chapter 10. Inequality of Capital Ownership Addendum: Response to FT |url=http://piketty.pse.ens.fr/files/capital21c/en/Piketty2014TechnicalAppendixResponsetoFT.pdf}}</ref><ref>Ryan Grim (May 29, 2014). [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/05/29/thomas-piketty-response_n_5412036.html Thomas Piketty Rebuts FT Charges: 'Criticism For The Sake Of Criticism']. ''[[The Huffington Post]].'' Retrieved May 29, 2014.</ref>

A 2017 study in ''[[Social Science History]]'' by University of California Riverside economic historian [[Richard Sutch]] concluded "that Piketty's data for the wealth share of the top 10 percent for the period 1870 to 1970 are unreliable&nbsp;...  Piketty's data for the top 1 percent of the distribution for the nineteenth century (1810–1910) are also unreliable&nbsp;... The values Piketty reported for the twentieth century (1910–2010) are based on more solid ground, but have the disadvantage of muting the marked rise of inequality during the Roaring Twenties and the decline associated with the Great Depression."<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Sutch|first=Richard|date=2017|title=The One Percent across Two Centuries: A Replication of Thomas Piketty's Data on the Concentration of Wealth in the United States|journal=Social Science History|volume=41|issue=4|pages=587–613|doi=10.1017/ssh.2017.27|issn=0145-5532|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Awards and honours==
* 2014 [[Financial Times and McKinsey Business Book of the Year Award]]<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.ft.com/intl/cms/s/0/b9e03c5c-6996-11e4-9f65-00144feabdc0.html |title=Thomas Piketty's 'Capital' wins Business Book of the Year |work=[[Financial Times]] |author=Andrew Hill |date=11 November 2014 |access-date=November 12, 2014}}</ref>
* 2014 [[National Book Critics Circle Award]] (General Nonfiction) finalist<ref name=nbcc2014>{{cite web|url=http://bookcritics.org/blog/archive/national-book-critics-circle-announces-its-finalists-for-publishing-year-20|title=National Book Critics Circle Announces Finalists for Publishing Year 2014|date=January 19, 2015|publisher=[[National Book Critics Circle]]|access-date=January 29, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150122224418/http://bookcritics.org/blog/archive/national-book-critics-circle-announces-its-finalists-for-publishing-year-20|archive-date=January 22, 2015|url-status=dead}}</ref>
* 2014 [[British Academy Medal]]<ref>{{cite web|title=British Academy Prizes and Medals Ceremony 2014|url= http://www.britac.ac.uk/events/british-academy-prizes-and-medals-ceremony-2014|website=[[British Academy]] |access-date=30 July 2017|date=25 November 2014}}</ref>

==Editions==
* ''Le Capital au XXI<sup>e</sup> siècle'', [[Éditions du Seuil]], Paris, 2013, {{ISBN|978-2021082289}} {{in lang|fr}} 
* ''Capital in the Twenty-First Century'', [[Harvard University Press]], 2014, {{ISBN|978-0674430006}}
